name: Parthenon
description: The Parthenon, a monumental Doric temple dedicated to the goddess Athena Parthenos (Athena the Virgin), stands as a testament to the architectural and artistic prowess of ancient Greece. Located on the Acropolis of Athens, it was constructed in the mid-5th century BCE, during the Golden Age of Pericles, replacing an older temple that had been destroyed by the Persians. The Parthenon served not only as a religious sanctuary but also as a treasury and a symbol of Athenian power and cultural dominance. Its meticulously crafted marble structure, adorned with intricate sculptures and friezes depicting mythological scenes, represents the pinnacle of classical Greek architecture, embodying ideals of harmony, proportion, and balance. The building's design incorporated optical refinements to enhance its aesthetic appeal, showcasing the advanced engineering and mathematical understanding of the ancient Greeks. Over the centuries, the Parthenon has endured various transformations, serving as a church, a mosque, and even a gunpowder storage facility, leading to significant damage. Despite its partial ruin, the Parthenon remains a potent symbol of Western civilization and a major tourist attraction, inspiring awe and admiration for its enduring beauty and historical significance.
subtitle: Temple of Athena on the Acropolis
significance: The Parthenon was the main temple dedicated to Athena, the patron goddess of Athens, and a symbol of Athenian power and cultural achievement. It housed a massive gold and ivory statue of Athena Parthenos and served as the city's treasury.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Athena
keyMyths.description: Athena, goddess of wisdom, warfare, and crafts, was born fully formed from the head of Zeus. Hephaestus, or Prometheus in some accounts, split Zeus's head open with an axe to relieve his severe headache, and Athena sprang forth, clad in armor. This miraculous birth established Athena as a powerful and respected deity, closely associated with her father Zeus and embodying intelligence and strategic prowess. The Parthenon was built in her honor, reflecting her importance to the city of Athens.

keyMyths.title: Athena's Contest with Poseidon
keyMyths.description: Athena and Poseidon both vied for patronage of the city of Athens. Poseidon struck the Acropolis with his trident, creating a saltwater spring (or a horse in some versions). Athena offered an olive tree, symbolizing peace and prosperity. The Athenians chose Athena's gift, recognizing its long-term benefits, and thus the city was named after her. This myth explains Athena's role as the protector of Athens and highlights the importance of wisdom and foresight.

keyMyths.title: The Panathenaic Festival
keyMyths.description: The Panathenaic Festival was a major Athenian celebration held every four years in honor of Athena. It involved athletic competitions, musical performances, and a grand procession that culminated in the presentation of a newly woven peplos (robe) to the statue of Athena Parthenos inside the Parthenon. The festival reinforced Athenian identity and celebrated the city's devotion to its patron goddess. Depictions of the Panathenaic procession can be found on the Parthenon frieze.
keyMyths.source: Various Athenian inscriptions and literary sources
